title: Schankian Primitives as Manifold-Local Operators
subtitle: What If ATRANS Means Something Different in Every Register?

children.children: Schank assumed his primitives were universal. But what if they're local — varying across a manifold of human speech?

children: This tutorial explores the idea that ATRANS, PTRANS, and other conceptual primitives aren't fixed operations but sections of a fiber bundle, changing their behavior as you move across registers, sociolects, and modalities. 'She gave him the cold shoulder' and 'The defendant transferred custody' both involve ATRANS — but the operator acts differently in each world.

children.children: Schank's Conceptual Dependency theory (1975) proposed that all human language can be decomposed into a small set of primitive actions:

children.definition: Abstract transfer — change of possession or control. 'Give,' 'sell,' 'donate,' 'transfer,' 'steal.'

children.children: The key assumption: these primitives are UNIVERSAL. ATRANS is ATRANS whether you're describing a street market in Lagos, a corporate merger in Manhattan, or a diplomatic exchange in Geneva. The surface words change; the underlying operator does not.
But is this actually true?
Consider these sentences, all involving ATRANS (transfer of possession/control):
🏛️ Legal: 'The defendant transferred custody of the minor to the state.' → ATRANS(defendant, state, custody) — formal, bounded by legal definitions, reversible only through court action
🗣️ Casual: 'She gave him the cold shoulder.' → ATRANS(she, him, rejection??) — metaphorical, the 'object' being transferred is an emotional state, not a physical thing
🎵 AAVE: 'He stay giving people the business.' → ATRANS(he, people, criticism) — habitual aspect marker 'stay' modifies the operator's temporal profile; 'the business' is culturally specific
📜 Literary: 'She bestowed upon him the honor of her confidence.' → ATRANS(she, him, confidence) — the directionality is marked by 'bestow upon,' implying a power asymmetry absent in casual 'give'

In each case, something is being 'transferred.' But the selectional restrictions (what CAN be transferred), the argument types (who can be giver/receiver), the composability rules (what other operators can combine), and even the temporal profile (one-time vs. habitual) are ALL different. Is it really the same operator?
Here's the geometric reframing: imagine all of human speech as a surface — a manifold. Different regions of this surface correspond to different ways of using language:

Think of the speech manifold like the Earth's surface. In the Amazon, the rules for survival involve rivers, canopy, and humidity. In the Sahara, the rules involve sand, heat, and oases. The SAME law of physics (gravity, thermodynamics) applies everywhere — but the local manifestation is completely different.

Similarly, ATRANS (transfer) is a 'law' that applies everywhere on the speech manifold. But in the legal region, it manifests as formal custody transfer with strict argument types. In the AAVE region, it manifests as habitual interpersonal exchange with culturally specific objects. Same law, different local expression.

props.title: What Is a Fiber Bundle? (The Math)
props.subtitle: For the geometrically curious

children.children: A fiber bundle is a mathematical structure with three parts:

children.definition: The manifold of speech registers/contexts. Each point is a 'location' — standard English, AAVE, legal, literary, etc.

At each point on the base space, there's a 'fiber' — the space of possible operator behaviors at that location. The fiber over 'legal English' contains the legal version of ATRANS; the fiber over 'AAVE' contains the AAVE version.

A section is a smooth choice of one point from each fiber. ATRANS is a section: at every point on the manifold, it picks out a specific operator behavior. But the picked behavior VARIES as you move across the manifold.

children.children: The key insight: ATRANS isn't a point in operator space — it's a SECTION of a fiber bundle. It's a function from manifold locations to operator behaviors. When Schank said ATRANS is universal, he was saying the section is flat (constant). We're proposing it has curvature.
This is an empirical question! Here are three lines of evidence suggesting operators are NOT universal:

props.title: Evidence 1: Selectional Restrictions Change
props.subtitle: What can be 'given' depends on where you are

children.children: In legal English, ATRANS applies to: custody, rights, property, authority, liability. In casual English, ATRANS applies to: objects, favors, information, emotional states ('give someone a hard time'). In AAVE, ATRANS applies to: social actions ('give someone the business'), emotional states, and habitual patterns that standard English doesn't grammaticalize.

The ARGUMENT TYPES are different. Legal ATRANS requires arguments with legal standing. Casual ATRANS accepts metaphorical objects. AAVE ATRANS can be aspectually modified in ways standard English can't express ('stay giving' = habitual ATRANS). These aren't just different words for the same thing — they're different operations.

props.title: Evidence 2: Composition Rules Change
props.subtitle: How operators combine depends on register

children.children: In legal English: ATRANS∘ATRANS is a 'chain of title' (I transfer to you, you transfer to them — each link is documented). In casual English: ATRANS∘ATRANS is 're-gifting' (a single compound concept with social stigma). In AAVE: ATRANS∘ATRANS might be 'passing it along' with implications of community circulation that neither legal nor standard English captures.
The COMPOSITION is register-dependent. The same operator sequence produces semantically different compound operations depending on manifold location.

props.title: Evidence 3: Our Own Operator Discovery Finds Variation
props.subtitle: Wake-sleep compression on different corpora

children.children: In our operator discovery work (wake-sleep library learning), we recovered 4 Schankian primitives + 2 novel ones from ATOMIC event descriptions. But ATOMIC is predominantly standard English, middle-class scenarios. What happens if we run the same algorithm on AAVE text? On legal opinions? On literary fiction?

Prediction: if operators are truly universal, the same library should emerge regardless of corpus. If operators are manifold-local, different corpora should produce different libraries — or the same operator names with different composition rules and selectional restrictions.
This idea connects directly to the Hydra architecture — a multi-head linguistic preprocessing system where each head is a 'grounding axis' that maps words into world-coordinates:

props.title: How Hydra Would Handle Register-Dependent Operators

children.children: Step 1: The REGISTER HEAD reads the context and outputs a manifold coordinate z. ('This text is in AAVE → z = z_aave')
Step 2: The MANIFOLD-AWARE EMBEDDING LAYER adjusts token embeddings based on z. ('bad' at z_aave gets the positive-valence embedding, not the negative one)

Step 3: The THEMATIC HEAD assigns operator-argument structure. But which operators are available, and what arguments they accept, depends on z. (At z_aave, 'give someone the business' maps to ATRANS with a culturally-specific object argument)
Step 4: The BODY operates on fully grounded tokens — correct embedding, correct register, correct operator semantics.

The register head doesn't just affect word meanings — it affects OPERATOR meanings. The thematic head can't do its job without knowing which version of ATRANS applies. This is why register detection must come BEFORE parsing in the Hydra pipeline.

If operators are manifold-local, the grounding problem gets more interesting. Being 'grounded' doesn't just mean knowing what ATRANS does — it means knowing what ATRANS does HERE, in this register, in this context, for this community of speakers.

'When in Rome, do as the Romans do.' You think you're in one fiber (standard English, where 'bad' means bad) but you're actually in another (AAVE, where 'bad' means good). The rules are different in non-obvious but compounding ways. A garden-path sentence is exactly this: you committed to the wrong fiber, and the rules you're following are almost-but-not-quite right until they catastrophically diverge.
This dissolves the grounding problem from 'does the system understand?' into 'does the system know where it is on the manifold?' — which is empirically testable, dimension by dimension.
Here's a concrete experimental plan:

children.definition: Fine-tune GPT-2 on register-specific corpora (AAVE, legal, literary). Measure per-token embedding drift. Tokens with high drift are 'curved' — their meaning varies across the manifold. This is running NOW on our T4 GPU.
Run wake-sleep operator discovery separately on each register corpus. Compare the discovered libraries. Same operators = universal. Different operators = manifold-local.
For operators that appear in multiple libraries, compare their composition rules. Does ATRANS∘PTRANS mean the same thing in legal vs. casual English? (Legal: 'convey and deliver.' Casual: 'mail it.')
For shared operators, compare what arguments they accept. Does legal ATRANS accept the same entity types as AAVE ATRANS?

children.children: If operators really are manifold-local, then the speech manifold has topological structure that we can discover. Different registers aren't just 'different ways of talking' — they're different geometric neighborhoods with different local rules.

And like Dirac studying his equation and discovering that the structure REQUIRED antimatter to exist, we might discover that the topology of the speech manifold REQUIRES certain registers or operator variants to exist — locations on the manifold that the math predicts but that no linguist has named.
The ultimate deliverable isn't a language model. It's a MAP OF THE MANIFOLD — the shape of human meaning-space, empirically derived, with predictions about regions no one has visited yet.

questions.question: Why might ATRANS be considered 'manifold-local' rather than universal?
options: Because different languages have different words for 'give'
Because the selectional restrictions, argument types, and composition rules of ATRANS vary across registers
Because ATRANS doesn't exist in some languages
Because Schank was wrong about everything
questions.explanation: The key insight is that ATRANS isn't just a label — it has specific behavior (what can be transferred, who can participate, how it composes with other operators). These behaviors change across registers, making it a section of a fiber bundle rather than a fixed point.

questions.question: In the fiber bundle framework, what is a 'section'?
options: A chapter of a linguistics textbook
A constant operator that never changes
A smooth choice of one behavior from each fiber across the entire manifold
The base space of the bundle
questions.explanation: A section picks one point from the fiber at each location on the base manifold. ATRANS-as-a-section means: at every register/context, ATRANS picks out a specific operator behavior — but that behavior varies smoothly as you move across registers.

questions.question: Why must the register head come BEFORE the thematic head in Hydra?
options: Because register detection is computationally cheaper
Because thematic roles don't exist in some registers
Because which operators exist and what arguments they accept depends on the register
Because the register head needs thematic information as input
questions.explanation: If operators are manifold-local, the thematic head needs to know which VERSION of ATRANS applies before it can assign argument roles. The register head provides the manifold coordinate that determines the local operator inventory.
